questions.question: The electrical power dissipated by a component is given by:
questions.explanation: $P = VI$ — power equals voltage multiplied by current. The SI unit is the watt (W).

questions.question: A current of $3.0\text{ A}$ flows through a component with $12\text{ V}$ across it. What is the power dissipated?
A current of $2.0\text{ A}$ flows through a $5.0\text{ }Ω$ resistor. What is the power dissipated?
A $24\text{ V}$ battery is connected across a $12\text{ }Ω$ resistor. What is the power dissipated?
A lamp rated at $60\text{ W}$ operates at $240\text{ V}$. What current does it draw?
A resistor dissipates $100\text{ W}$ when carrying $5.0\text{ A}$. What is its resistance?
A $36\text{ W}$ device has a resistance of $4.0\text{ }Ω$. What voltage is applied across it?
A $100\text{ W}$ lamp is left on for $5.0$ hours. How much electrical energy does it consume in kWh?
A $2.0\text{ kW}$ heater runs for $3.0$ hours. If electricity costs $\$0.15$ per kWh, what is the cost?
A resistor connected to a $6\text{ V}$ battery dissipates $12\text{ W}$. What power does it dissipate when connected to a $12\text{ V}$ battery?
questions.explanation: $P \propto V^2$. Doubling $V$ quadruples $P$: $12 \times 4 = 48\text{ W}$. Or: $R = 36/12 = 3\text{ }Ω$, $P = 144/3 = 48\text{ W}$.

questions.question: Two identical $10\text{ }Ω$ resistors are connected in series to a $20\text{ V}$ battery. What is the total power dissipated?
The same two $10\text{ }Ω$ resistors are connected in parallel to the same $20\text{ V}$ battery. What is the total power dissipated?
questions.explanation: $R_{total} = 5\text{ }Ω$. $P = V^2/R = 400/5 = 80\text{ W}$. Parallel connection draws more power than series.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered electrical power and energy calculations!
scoreMessages.70: Good work! Review $P = I^2R$, $P = V^2/R$, and energy cost calculations.
scoreMessages.50: Keep practicing! Focus on choosing the right power formula and converting to kWh.
scoreMessages.0: Review the basics: $P = IV$, $P = I^2R$, $P = V^2/R$, $E = Pt$, and cost calculations.
